content.assessment: A national viability registry and transparency framework is, from a governance-design perspective, a comparatively low-authority-conflict intervention since it does not itself reassign maintainer authority, rights, or accountability — it is explicitly framed as non-mandating. However, its practical value depends on whether it can penetrate a governance structure characterized by centralized, non-transparent administrative discretion: closures currently proceed via administrative acts without a dedicated statutory procedure (F-live-legal_and_governance-04), oversight sits with the Interior Ministry rather than an education-focused body (F-live-legal_and_governance-03), and existing accountability for reversing decisions is informal/political rather than procedural (F-live-legal_and_governance-08). A registry could formalize the transport-responsibility ambiguity the ombudsman identified (F-live-legal_and_governance-05) by making the resulting gaps visible and trackable, which is a meaningful governance contribution even without new authority. But because the registry has no enforcement power, its influence depends entirely on whether central decision-makers choose to consult it, which past patterns of political-administrative override suggest cannot be assumed.
opportunities: Could be attached to the 2026 restructuring effort as a low-friction transparency component that complements the restoration of director/municipal input without requiring new authority transfer (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
strengths: Does not require reallocating maintainer authority or altering rights, making it comparatively easy to implement within the existing centralized structure without direct legal conflict

Could formally document and track the specific transport-responsibility ambiguity already flagged by the ombudsman, converting an informal finding into an institutionalized monitoring function (F-live-legal_and_governance-05)

Could provide an evidentiary basis to evaluate the informal reversal pattern (political-administrative intervention after protest) and potentially formalize it into codified criteria over time (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
threats: Given the demonstrated pattern of decisions being reversed only through informal political channels rather than codified procedure, a purely informational registry may be systematically bypassed by the same discretionary decision-making it seeks to inform (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
weaknesses: As a non-mandating framework, it lacks enforceability and creates no new accountability obligation on the district centers or the Interior Ministry-led oversight structure (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)
Does not address the core legal gap of an absent dedicated closure procedure; it would document ad hoc practice rather than replace it (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
